serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of shredded ginger as a condiment or garnish is approximately 10 g, equivalent to a small mound of fine julienned strips served alongside dumplings or dim sum.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger root, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169231)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw ginger root used directly. Shredded ginger is raw ginger root cut into thin strips; no cooking or processing alters the nutrient profile materially. Values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ginger root is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of approximately 15 is widely cited in peer-reviewed literature for raw ginger root based on its low available carbohydrate content and high fibre and bioactive compound profile. Glycemic load per 10 g serving = (15 × 1.777 g available carbohydrate per 10 g) / 100 ≈ 0.3, classified as negligible. GI category: low (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (13 mg per 100 g) and presence of potassium (415 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Shredded ginger is very low in sodium and provides potassium, making it a hypertension-friendly condiment when used in typical small serving sizes of 5–15 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (approximately 15) and small serving size limiting glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of less than 0.5 per 10 g condiment serving, shredded ginger poses negligible glycemic impact and may be consumed freely by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; emerging evidence also suggests gingerols may modestly improve insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory gingerol compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shredded ginger contains negligible saturated fat (0.20 g per 100 g) and no cholesterol, and its bioactive gingerols and shogaols have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties in clinical studies, supporting its use as a cardioprotective condiment.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shredded ginger (姜丝, jiāng sī) is fresh ginger root (Zingiber officinale) that has been peeled and cut into fine julienned strips. In Chinese and Singaporean cuisine, it is used as a condiment, garnish, and flavouring agent, most commonly served alongside steamed dumplings such as Xiao Long Bao, steamed fish, and various dim sum dishes. The strips deliver a sharp, pungent, and slightly spicy flavour with a fibrous, crisp texture. Nutritionally, raw ginger root provides approximately 80 kcal per 100 g, with 17.8 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g protein, and 0.75 g fat. It is a meaningful source of potassium (415 mg per 100 g) and contains dietary fibre (2 g per 100 g). Its sodium content is very low at 13 mg per 100 g. Ginger has a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Bioactive compounds including gingerols, shogaols, and zingerone contribute anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential insulin-sensitising effects. As a condiment served in small quantities (typically 5–15 g), its caloric and macronutrient contribution to a meal is minimal. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ginger strips?
What is the GI of ginger?
meal_context: Condiment served with Xiao Long Bao (steamed pork soup dumplings)
Ingredient in Chinese soups and broths

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169231) for raw ginger root, which is an exact match for the food as served (shredded raw ginger). GI estimated from peer-reviewed literature consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Multilingual names verified against standard Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi lexicons.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; ginger root contains no industrially produced trans fats (naturally zero). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for ginger root. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; ginger root does not contain vitamin D. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Grains and staples', which is incorrect for a fresh aromatic condiment; corrected to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' per standard food classification. The description in the input JSON referenced supply constraints specific to a restaurant context; this has been replaced with a factual food description. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as ginger root is not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database; this is documented in gi_source. Serving size set to 10 g reflecting typical condiment portion served with dim sum.
